critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Spring-fed woodland grazer shifting from winter browse to tender new growth.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer flash the white underside of their tails to warn others of danger nearby.
critters.activeFact: At this point in spring, deer are often visible along trail edges and openings where the first fresh plants are coming up.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Recently emerged forest forager searching for the easiest calories on the landscape.
critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent memory and can remember food locations for years.
critters.activeFact: In early April, bear movement can start picking up around swamps, low ground, and quiet woodland feeding areas.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean spring hunter covering open ground and edge habitat with purpose.
critters.fact: Coyotes can adapt to almost any environment, from deserts to cities, making them highly versatile hunters.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes are often using muddy two-tracks, trail corridors, and open field edges during low-light hours.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Quick-footed edge hunter working brush, grass, and exposed spring ground.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance and to keep warm in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: In early April, red foxes may be spotted crossing open fields or pausing near grassy pockets to listen for movement.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-loving woodland fox staying mostly tucked into cover as spring advances.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are excellent climbers, often climbing trees to escape predators or find food.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, gray foxes are more likely to stay ghostlike in brushy cover than show themselves in open ground.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Stealth predator slipping through timber and brush in the muddy spring season.
critters.fact: Bobcats have excellent night vision, allowing them to hunt effectively in low light conditions.
critters.activeFact: In early April, bobcats may follow brush lines and wooded transitions where rabbit and squirrel activity is increasing.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow spring browser still relying on bark, buds, and woody foods.
critters.fact: Porcupines cannot shoot their quills; quills detach only when predators touch them.
critters.activeFact: At this stage of spring, porcupines are still likely to be found feeding in trees or near woody browse sites.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Busy water engineer dealing with moving spring currents and opened channels.
critters.fact: Beavers build impressive dams that create wetlands, benefiting many other species and ecosystems.
critters.activeFact: In early April, new cuttings, muddy slides, and repaired flow spots can all point to active beaver work.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Wetland rodent more exposed now that ice and shoreline barriers are gone.
critters.fact: Muskrats build underwater entrances to their lodges, keeping them safe from predators and harsh weather.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may be swimming shoreline routes or feeding near cattails and shallow emergent plants.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Sharp little shoreline predator working every opening along creek and pond edges.
critters.fact: Minks have waterproof fur that helps them swim efficiently while hunting underwater.
critters.activeFact: In early April, mink often patrol muddy water margins where movement and scent are concentrated.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Wide-ranging forest carnivore threading through spring-softening timber country.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and use trees to hunt and escape predators in forests.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, fishers may still move mostly unseen through heavier timber and connected forest edges.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Playful water hunter thriving now that streams and pond edges are opening wide.
critters.fact: River otters can hold their breath underwater for up to eight minutes while hunting for food.
critters.activeFact: In early April, otters may cruise channels, slide muddy banks, and work open water for fish.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Active treetop forager balancing nest work, feeding, and chasing behavior.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels use over 10,000 vocalizations to communicate with each other in the wild.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are often highly visible on warm days, racing through trees and carrying nesting material.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny woodland loudmouth still guarding food and personal space with full commitment.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their food caches from other squirrels year-round.
critters.activeFact: In early April, red squirrels often seem louder than ever as they defend middens and patrol nearby trees.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Small ground squirrel back in regular circulation on sunny spring days.
critters.fact: Chipmunks use their cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, chipmunks are often darting around logs, rocks, and burrow entrances collecting food.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Northern forest hare caught in the awkward handoff between snow season and bare ground.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally for camouflage, turning white in winter and brown in summer.
critters.activeFact: In early April, patchy camouflage can make snowshoe hares more noticeable where cover is thinning.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Common spring rabbit feeding along hedges, brush piles, and fresh green margins.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails can run up to 18 miles per hour to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails are often most visible along yard edges, hedgerows, and brushy openings.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-roaming opportunist taking fuller advantage of milder spring conditions.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws that help them open containers and catch prey efficiently.
critters.activeFact: In early April, raccoons may be covering more ground around creeks, ponds, camps, and woodland edges.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive scavenger benefiting from softer nights and easier travel.
critters.fact: Opossums are the only North American marsupials, carrying and nursing their young in pouches.
critters.activeFact: At this point in spring, opossums may be seen more often around brush, sheds, and edge habitat after dark.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Wandering spring forager still putting on miles during the active breeding window.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a foul odor up to 10 feet to defend themselves from predators.
critters.activeFact: In early April, skunks may show up more often along roadsides, field edges, and open damp ground.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Night-gliding squirrel quietly active above the woods while most eyes are elsewhere.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide using a special membrane called a patagium stretched between their limbs.
critters.activeFact: Even now, flying squirrels remain mostly unseen unless activity near feeders or nest boxes gives them away.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Early-season insect hunter beginning to benefit from warmer dusk conditions.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects, emitting high-pitched sounds inaudible to humans.
critters.activeFact: On a mild early April night, little brown bats may make some of the season’s first steady feeding passes.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tough spring bat ready to work mild evenings before many others fully ramp up.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats can live over 19 years, using echolocation to hunt insects in complete darkness.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warmer evenings near lights or buildings can bring big brown bats into view.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Spring-voiced woodland owl still calling through leafless hardwood nights.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinctive hoots that sound like "Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?"
critters.activeFact: In early April, barred owl calls can still carry far through the woods, especially near dusk and dawn.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful early nester already well ahead of the rest of spring’s family schedule.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls have ear tufts that help with camouflage and communication, not hearing.
critters.activeFact: At this stage of spring, great horned owls may be more focused on guarding and feeding young than courting.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Loud, sharp-eyed spring bird acting increasingly territorial around food and nesting space.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic hawk calls to scare away other birds and protect their food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays may be noisier, bossier, and quicker to chase off rivals around feeders and tree cover.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Brilliant red singer now fully planted in the heart of spring territory season.
critters.fact: Northern Cardinals are known for their bright red color and distinctive, melodious songs heard year-round.
critters.activeFact: In early April, cardinals are often among the first loud voices of the morning around brush and woodland edges.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny spring dynamo mixing feeder runs with cavity checks and pair behavior.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of hiding spots for their food throughout the winter.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, chickadees may shift from nonstop winter feeding to more courtship-like calling and chasing.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious woodland songbird sounding off more often as nest season nears.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice use a unique “peter-peter-peter” call to communicate and establish territory.
critters.activeFact: In early April, titmice may spend more time calling from exposed branches than they did in deep winter.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Bark-running spring bird still busy on trunks while quietly shifting toward nesting mode.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches walk headfirst down tree trunks to find hidden insects and seeds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches may be especially active on larger trunks, often calling more between feeding stops.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small drummer using spring sound to claim space and prepare for nesting.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers use their stiff tail feathers for balance while pecking on tree trunks.
critters.activeFact: In early April, sharp little bursts of tapping often mean a downy is making its spring presence known.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Larger bark specialist now louder as breeding-season urgency builds.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from deep tree bark crevices.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, hairy woodpeckers may be easier to detect by sound than by sight in taller timber.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Big forest carpenter announcing spring with heavier voice and harder hammering.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large rectangular holes in trees to find insects and build nests.
critters.activeFact: In early April, deep pounding and loud calls can reveal pileated woodpeckers long before you see them.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Vocal woodland woodpecker becoming more animated as spring competition rises.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers drum loudly to defend territory and attract mates with rapid, rhythmic tapping.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-bellied woodpeckers may spend more time chasing and calling than quietly feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced dove settling into pair behavior and early nest attempts.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour to escape predators quickly.
critters.activeFact: In early April, paired doves may already be selecting spots and giving more frequent cooing displays.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Late-brightening finch beginning its slow spring color and energy shift.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice annually, changing from bright yellow summer to duller winter plumage.
critters.activeFact: At this stage, goldfinches may still look partly winter-worn but noticeably livelier around feeders and edges.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-season ground feeder still hanging on while spring keeps pressing forward.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos often form large flocks during winter, enhancing safety and foraging success.
critters.activeFact: In early April, juncos may still dominate the ground under feeders before later seasonal movement picks up.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social finch growing more musical and pair-focused as spring develops.
critters.fact: House Finches were once sold as "Hollywood Finches" to bypass pet laws in California.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches may be shifting from casual feeder visits to more obvious singing and pairing behavior.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland finch tied to lingering seed sources and shifting spring movement.
critters.fact: Male Purple Finches have a raspberry-red head and chest, unlike the females' streaky brown coloring.
critters.activeFact: In early April, purple finch appearances can be brief and sporadic, showing up in little waves.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Big ground bird now leaning hard into spring strut, display, and dominance behavior.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can run up to 20 miles per hour and fly short distances at speeds up to 55 mph.
critters.activeFact: By early April, gobblers may be strutting more often in openings, field edges, and sunny woodland pockets.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest drummer building momentum as spring display season gains real traction.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create "dust baths" to keep feathers clean and remove parasites.
critters.activeFact: In early April, that low thumping rhythm in the woods may be happening more often than just a week ago.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland returnee bringing full spring energy back to marsh and meadow country.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform elaborate group dances to strengthen social bonds and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, their rattling calls and slow wingbeats can be one of the most unmistakable signs of the season.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Sharp-minded spring generalist patrolling fields, roads, and woodland margins.
critters.fact: American Crows can recognize human faces and remember individuals who pose threats.
critters.activeFact: In early April, crows are often loudly active at sunrise and quick to investigate anything unusual.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large spring corvid cruising ridges and openings with steady pair coordination.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic human speech and other sounds from their environment with impressive accuracy.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, ravens may be seen soaring together, calling over timber, and working the same routes repeatedly.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Big soaring raptor balancing nest work with watchful hunting over open country.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use their sharp vision to spot prey from over a mile away while soaring high.
critters.activeFact: In early April, some red-tails may spend more time circling and nest-tending than sitting on roadside perches.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland chaser getting sharper and more defensive as nesting season advances.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are expert flyers, weaving skillfully through trees to catch birds mid-air.
critters.activeFact: Right now, quick darting flights through trees can sometimes be a Cooper’s hawk policing its spring space.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Common spring duck spread across open water, ditches, ponds, and flooded edges.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can sleep with one eye open to stay alert for predators.
critters.activeFact: In early April, mallards may be feeding and resting in shallow flooded areas that barely existed a month ago.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Bright spring duck using quiet flooded timber and cavity-rich wetland edges.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities and are one of the few ducks with colorful, iridescent plumage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be tucked into sheltered bends, flooded woods, or backwater pockets.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Territorial spring goose claiming shoreline space and setting up for nesting.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly over 1,500 miles during migration without stopping.
critters.activeFact: In early April, geese may be especially loud and stubborn around ponds, marsh edges, and open fields.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Tall wader back on the hunt along open creeks, ponds, and marsh margins.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons can patiently stand still for minutes while hunting fish in shallow water.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, a lone heron standing statue-still near a shoreline is a classic early spring sight.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Sun-seeking turtle starting to claim logs and banks on warm spring afternoons.
critters.fact: Painted Turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, helping them stay submerged longer.
critters.activeFact: In early April, the first busy basking logs may start filling whenever the sun sticks around.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Heavy-bodied aquatic turtle beginning its slow return to more active spring movement.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles can live over 30 years and have powerful jaws that snap shut in less than a second.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may just be starting to shift more often in shallower or warming water.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Shallow-curious panfish responding to longer days and slowly warming spring water.
critters.fact: Bluegill sunfish can rapidly change color to communicate and blend into their environment.
critters.activeFact: In early April, bluegill often hold near weeds, wood, or dark-bottom shallows that warm a bit faster.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Spring-moving predator easing toward staging zones around warming cover.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can swallow prey up to half their own body length in a single gulp!
critters.activeFact: At this stage, bass may be edging toward cover-rich shallows without fully committing to spawn areas yet.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Early-active schooling fish hitting one of its better spring windows.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch can change sex from female to male in response to environmental conditions.
critters.activeFact: In early April, perch schools may be moving more predictably through accessible nearshore water.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Big spring predator working shallow wet ground during one of its key seasonal periods.
critters.fact: Northern Pike can grow over 4 feet long and use ambush tactics to catch prey swiftly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may be drawn into soft shallow habitat that looked empty all winter.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny chorus frog ready to turn warm evenings into a full spring soundtrack.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers produce a loud, high-pitched peep to attract mates during early spring nights.
critters.activeFact: In early April, one warm wet evening can make peeper calls suddenly explode across a marsh or low wet spot.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond frog still waiting for warmer water before joining the louder spring crowd.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce loud, deep calls heard up to a mile away in wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs are often still quieter and less obvious than peepers or other early amphibians.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline frog starting to stir as wet edges and shallow water gain warmth.
critters.fact: Green frogs communicate using distinctive croaks that sound like a loose banjo string twang.
critters.activeFact: In early April, green frogs may start appearing near thawed shorelines and quiet wet pockets.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Early-moving spotted frog taking advantage of mild wetland warmups.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs can jump over 20 times their body length to escape predators swiftly.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, leopard frogs may show up near sunny puddled ground, shallow pools, and marsh edges.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: One of spring’s first snakes, emerging anywhere the sun can warm cover quickly.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can survive underwater by trapping air in their lungs for extended dives.
critters.activeFact: In early April, a sunny afternoon can bring garter snakes out along trails, edges, and warm debris piles.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic snake easing toward activity as shorelines and shallows warm up.
critters.fact: Water snakes can hold their breath underwater for up to 30 minutes while hunting or hiding.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny banks near calm open water may offer the best chance of seeing limited movement.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive woodland snake beginning to reappear around sun-warmed shelter cover.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes mimic venomous snakes with their red, black, and white banding to avoid predators.
critters.activeFact: In early April, milk snakes may turn up near barns, woodpiles, and brushy cover on mild afternoons.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Northbound migrant still mostly advancing toward, not yet filling, northern Michigan.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles to winter in specific Mexican forests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarchs are more a sign of the season approaching than a common local butterfly sight.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big spring butterfly still waiting on steadier warmth and more reliable blooming plants.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies have distinctive tail-like wing extensions resembling a swallow's tail for camouflage.
critters.activeFact: At this point, swallowtails are still more likely to be absent than actively moving through local woods.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Future aerial hunter still mostly living underwater while spring gets established.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly up to 30 miles per hour and hover like tiny helicopters.
critters.activeFact: In early April, wetlands may look quiet above the surface while future dragonflies are growing below it.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender wetland insect still mostly hidden in underwater form this early in the season.
critters.fact: Damselflies fold their wings along their body when resting, unlike dragonflies that keep them open.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies are more likely tucked into pond margins below the surface than hovering above them.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer light-maker still dormant below the surface while spring lays groundwork.
critters.fact: Fireflies produce light through a chemical reaction called bioluminescence to attract mates and ward off predators.
critters.activeFact: In early April, the woods may feel alive again, but firefly nights are still waiting farther down the trail.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Early pollinator taking every decent warm window spring is willing to offer.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by dancing to share the locations of flowers with their hive mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny breaks can briefly put honeybees in the air even when mornings still start cold.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Cold-tough queen bee out early and searching for both flowers and a future nest site.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can regulate their body temperature, allowing them to fly in cooler weather than most insects.
critters.activeFact: In early April, a single large bumblebee drifting low across the ground is often a queen scouting the season.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Still mostly absent from the scene while spring focuses on wetter and earlier risers.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers can jump over 20 times their body length using powerful hind legs.
critters.activeFact: At this point in the year, spring is still building the stage long before grasshoppers start hopping through it.
